text.title: DDD Repositories Failure Modes
text.summary: Four antipatterns observed when implementing repositories. Symptom + root cause + fix per item.

antipattern.symptom: `from sqlalchemy.orm import Session` inside `domain/orders/repository.py`. Domain test suite needs DB to run.
antipattern.root-cause: Interface declared in domain but referenced ORM types in signatures.
antipattern.fix: Replace `Session` with the domain port + use DI to inject the session into the adapter only. Domain interface methods take pure domain types.

antipattern.symptom: `OrderRepository.find_orders_for_customer_in_date_range(...)` proliferates: 30 different finder methods cluttered with optional filters.
antipattern.fix: Move to `OrderQueryService` (or a Read Model). Repository keeps only `find_by_id` + `save` + `delete`.

antipattern.symptom: Repository returns an ORM model (or aggregate holding ORM relationships). Caller iterates `order.items` outside the session; lazy loader throws `DetachedInstanceError`.

root-cause.source: ORM lazy loading + DDD return contract

antipattern.root-cause: Repository didn't materialize relationships; ORM model exposed across boundary.
antipattern.fix: Inside the adapter, eager-load relations + map to a domain aggregate. Return the aggregate. ORM model never crosses the boundary.

antipattern.symptom: `OrderRepository` contains 800 LOC of mapping logic, includes business rules ("only return orders newer than 90 days"). Hard to test or replace.
antipattern.root-cause: Author dumped both mapping and policy into the adapter.
antipattern.fix: Extract mapping into a `OrderMapper`. Move policies onto the aggregate (`Order.is_archived`) or into a Domain Service. Adapter becomes a thin shell.
